summary: DHH endorses Dave Winer's criticism of Yahoo and Google's RSS reader interfaces, arguing that the 'combined view' — a single scrollable list of all posts — is far superior to three-paned email-style layouts. He credits this view as the reason he bought NetNewsWire and can efficiently skim 120 feeds. He argues that mimicking email interfaces for RSS is a misguided design choice rooted in overly literal metaphors.
key_points: Combined view (one long scrollable list) is the optimal interface for consuming aggregated RSS content
Three-paned email-style interfaces are an 'overly literal translation' that hinders RSS reading efficiency
The combined view enables skimming hundreds of articles in minutes, making high-volume feeds manageable
Bad UI metaphors — like early 80s 'virtual office' software with literal furniture — actively impede usability
money_quotes: Combined view, every thing in one long list, is the way to go for presenting aggregated content.

Fooling around with a three-paned view is like early 80'ties software that attempted to make your feel comfortable in the "virtual office" by having actual furniture, drawers, and other obstacles in your way.
It's known as an "overly literal translation" and it's a bad idea for making RSS "feel like email".
It's also the reason that I can stand being subscribed to 120 feeds and still skim through it in no time at all.
It's the reason I'm still subscribed to hyper-active bloggers like Scoble that can blog 50 things in a day and hit 2 I care about.
key_insight: New content formats deserve native interfaces designed for their strengths, not slavish imitations of older paradigms like email.
